quiz_questions.question_text: On a horizontal surface, what does the normal force equal for a stationary object?

options.text: The weight of the object (N = mg)

quiz_questions.explanation: On a horizontal surface with no other vertical forces, the normal force balances the weight: N = mg.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which direction does the normal force act?

options.text: Always toward the center of the Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is always perpendicular to the surface. On a flat floor it points up, but on an incline it points perpendicular to the incline surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an inclined plane, what is the component of gravity parallel to the surface?

options.text: mg sinθ, where θ is the angle of the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: The weight component along (parallel to) the incline = mg sinθ. This is what tends to make objects slide down.

quiz_questions.question_text: On an inclined plane, what is the normal force?

options.text: mg cosθ, where θ is the angle of the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force on an incline = mg cosθ. It equals the perpendicular component of gravity on the incline surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: As the angle of an incline increases from 0° to 90°, what happens to the normal force?

options.text: It decreases — N = mg cosθ, and cos decreases from 1 to 0

quiz_questions.explanation: N = mg cosθ. At 0° (flat): N = mg. At 90° (vertical): N = 0. The normal force continuously decreases.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does an object on a steeper incline tend to slide more easily?

options.text: The object weighs more on steeper inclines
Gravity is stronger on steeper inclines
The component of gravity along the incline (mg sinθ) increases while the normal force (and thus max friction) decreases

quiz_questions.explanation: As θ increases, mg sinθ (driving force) increases and mg cosθ (normal force, hence friction capacity) decreases. Both effects promote sliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg block sits on a 30° incline. What is the normal force? (g = 10 m/s²)
Can the normal force ever be greater than an object's weight?

options.text: Yes — if an additional force pushes the object into the surface, the normal force exceeds the weight
No — the normal force is always less than the weight
No — the normal force always equals the weight

quiz_questions.explanation: If you press down on a book on a table, the normal force must support both the weight and your push: N = mg + F_push. The normal force adjusts to prevent penetration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What angle of incline would give a normal force of zero?
quiz_questions.explanation: At 90°, the surface is vertical and there is no component of gravity pressing the object into the surface, so the normal force is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A box is on a ramp. Which force causes it to potentially slide down?

options.text: The component of gravity parallel to the ramp (mg sinθ)
The component of gravity perpendicular to the ramp

quiz_questions.explanation: mg sinθ acts down the ramp and is the driving force for sliding. It is opposed by friction (up the ramp).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the normal force?

options.text: A force that is always equal to the object's weight
A component of the gravitational force
A friction force perpendicular to motion
The contact force exerted by a surface on an object, directed perpendicular to the surface at the point of contact, preventing the object from penetrating the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is a reaction force from the surface. 'Normal' means perpendicular. It adjusts to whatever value is needed to prevent the object from passing through the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an inclined plane?

options.text: A flat surface tilted at an angle to the horizontal that allows the analysis of motion along and perpendicular to the surface using resolved weight components

quiz_questions.explanation: An inclined plane is one of the simple machines. In physics problems, it allows us to decompose gravity into components parallel and perpendicular to the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the angle of inclination?

options.text: The angle of the applied force
The angle (θ) between the inclined surface and the horizontal, which determines the split of gravitational force into parallel and perpendicular components
The angle between the object and the vertical

quiz_questions.explanation: θ is measured from the horizontal to the surface of the incline. It determines: parallel gravity = mg sinθ, perpendicular gravity = mg cosθ.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the parallel component of gravity on an incline?

options.text: The component of gravitational force directed along the surface of the incline, equal to mg sinθ, which tends to pull the object down the slope
The total weight of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel component (mg sinθ) acts along the slope surface, either accelerating the object downhill or being balanced by friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the perpendicular component of gravity on an incline?

options.text: The component of gravitational force directed into the surface of the incline, equal to mg cosθ, which is balanced by the normal force
The force pulling the object down the slope
The friction force on the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: The perpendicular component (mg cosθ) pushes the object into the surface. The normal force equals this component: N = mg cosθ (when no other forces act perpendicular to the surface).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the critical angle of an incline?

options.text: The steepest angle at which an object can remain stationary on an incline, determined by θ_c = arctan(μ_s) — beyond this angle, static friction cannot prevent sliding
The angle where the object flies off
The angle where normal force equals zero

quiz_questions.explanation: At the critical angle, mg sinθ = μ_s × mg cosθ → tanθ_c = μ_s. This is independent of mass. Beyond this angle, the object slides.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes weight decomposition on an incline?

options.text: The breakdown of an object over time
Dividing an object's mass into parts
The process of resolving the gravitational force vector into two perpendicular components: one parallel to the incline (mg sinθ) and one perpendicular to the incline (mg cosθ)

quiz_questions.explanation: Weight decomposition uses trigonometry to split gravity into useful components aligned with the incline. This simplifies the analysis of forces and acceleration on the ramp.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the free-body diagram for an object on an incline?

options.text: A diagram showing the object isolated with its weight (straight down), normal force (perpendicular to surface), friction (along surface), and any applied forces, typically using tilted axes aligned with the incline
A velocity-time graph for the incline
A picture of the incline from above
A diagram of all objects on the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: The FBD for an incline problem typically uses tilted axes (x along the slope, y perpendicular) so that the normal force and friction align with axes, requiring only the weight to be decomposed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes mechanical advantage of an inclined plane?

options.text: The normal force divided by the weight
The ratio of the weight of an object to the force needed to move it up the incline — a longer, gentler incline requires less force but over a greater distance
The friction coefficient of the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: For a frictionless incline: Force needed = mg sinθ, which is less than mg. The longer the incline (smaller θ), the smaller the force needed, but the greater the distance traveled. Work = same.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes equilibrium on an incline?

options.text: The condition where the net force on an object on an incline is zero — gravity's parallel component is balanced by friction (and/or an applied force), and the normal force balances the perpendicular component
An object that has reached the bottom of an incline
An object sliding down at constant velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium: ΣF_parallel = 0 (friction or applied force balances mg sinθ) and ΣF_perpendicular = 0 (N = mg cosθ). The object is either stationary or moving at constant velocity along the incline.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 10 kg box sits on a frictionless 30° incline. What is its acceleration down the slope? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 5 m/s² — a = g sinθ = 10 × sin30° = 10 × 0.5 = 5 m/s² down the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: On a frictionless incline: a = g sinθ = 10 × sin30° = 5 m/s² down the slope. The normal force and perpendicular gravity cancel.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 20 kg box is on a 45° incline with μ_k = 0.3. It slides down. What is its acceleration? (g = 10 m/s²)
A person pushes a 15 kg box up a 20° frictionless incline with a force applied parallel to the incline. What minimum force is needed to push it up at constant velocity?

options.text: 51.3 N — at constant velocity, F = mg sinθ = 15 × 10 × sin20° ≈ 15 × 10 × 0.342 ≈ 51.3 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity → a = 0 → F = mg sinθ = 150 × sin20° ≈ 150 × 0.342 ≈ 51.3 N up the incline.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child slides down a playground slide (incline angle 35°) at constant velocity. What is the coefficient of kinetic friction?

options.text: μ_k = tan35° ≈ 0.70 — constant velocity means mg sinθ = μ_k mg cosθ, so μ_k = tanθ

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant velocity → a = 0 → mg sinθ = μ_k mg cosθ → μ_k = sinθ/cosθ = tan35° ≈ 0.700.

quiz_questions.question_text: You place a coin on a book and slowly tilt the book. At 22° the coin starts to slide. What is the coefficient of static friction?

options.text: μ_s = tan22° ≈ 0.404 — the critical angle gives μ_s = tanθ_c

quiz_questions.explanation: At the critical angle: mg sinθ = μ_s mg cosθ → μ_s = tanθ_c = tan22° ≈ 0.404.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50 kg person stands at the center of a 4 m long plank resting on two supports at each end. What is the normal force at each support? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 250 N each — by symmetry, each support provides half the weight: N = mg/2 = 500/2 = 250 N

quiz_questions.explanation: By symmetry (person at center), each support bears half the weight. N₁ = N₂ = mg/2 = 500/2 = 250 N.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5 kg block on a 30° incline is attached by a rope to a 3 kg hanging block over a frictionless pulley. The incline is frictionless. Which way does the system move? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: The 3 kg block descends — weight of hanging block (30 N) > parallel force on inclined block (mg sin30° = 25 N), net force = 5 N, a = 5/8 = 0.625 m/s²
The 5 kg block slides down the incline

quiz_questions.explanation: Incline block: force down slope = 5(10)sin30° = 25 N. Hanging block: weight = 3(10) = 30 N. Since 30 > 25, the 3 kg block descends. a = (30 − 25)/(5 + 3) = 5/8 = 0.625 m/s².

quiz_questions.question_text: An object sits on a 60° incline. Without friction, what fraction of its weight acts along the slope?

options.text: sin60° ≈ 0.866 — about 86.6% of the weight acts down the slope

quiz_questions.explanation: The parallel component = mg sinθ. As a fraction of weight (mg): sinθ = sin60° ≈ 0.866, so about 86.6% of the weight drives the object down the slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery worker pushes a 40 kg box up a 15° ramp at constant velocity. Friction coefficient μ_k = 0.25. What force must she apply parallel to the ramp? (g = 10 m/s²)

Two inclines of angles 30° and 60° are placed back to back like a tent. A 2 kg block is placed on the 30° side and a 3 kg block on the 60° side, connected by a rope over the peak. Both sides are frictionless. Which way does the system accelerate? (g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: Both blocks slide down their respective sides
The 2 kg block slides down the 30° side
The 3 kg block on the 60° side slides down — its parallel weight (3 × 10 × sin60° ≈ 26 N) exceeds the 30° side (2 × 10 × sin30° = 10 N); a = (26 − 10)/5 = 3.2 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: 30° side: 2(10)sin30° = 10 N. 60° side: 3(10)sin60° ≈ 26 N. 60° side is heavier component. a = (26 − 10)/5 = 16/5 = 3.2 m/s² with the 3 kg block descending.
